1: Revelation of God. "A gentle answer turns away anger" — Christ embodies the gentle answer (maʿăneh rak) that disarms hostility. In his interactions with adversaries — the woman caught in adultery (John 8:1–11), the Samaritan woman (John 4), even the high priest's servant whose ear he restores (Luke 22:51) — he responds with calibrated gentleness. "I am gentle and humble in heart" (Matthew 11:29) is his self-description; the turning away of anger through gentleness is not a tactical choice but the expression of his character as the one in whom divine love takes flesh.
2: Revelation of God. "The tongue of the wise presents knowledge rightly" — Christ's teaching is the supreme instance of knowledge rightly presented. He "taught as one who had authority" (Matthew 7:29) — not just accurately but with the intrinsic authority of the one who is the source of the knowledge he communicates. The crowds who heard the Sermon on the Mount and "were amazed at his teaching" (Matthew 7:28) experienced the qualitative difference between wisdom correctly presented by the source and wisdom secondhand. He does not relay wisdom; he speaks from within it.
3: Revelation of God. "The LORD's eyes are everywhere, watching both the evil and the good" — Christ, who "knew what was in each person" (John 2:25) and who is described as having "eyes like blazing fire" (Revelation 1:14; Proverbs 2:18) in the letters to the seven churches, embodies the divine omniscience that this verse describes. His specific knowledge of the individual — Nathanael under the fig tree (John 1:48), the woman's five husbands (John 4:18), Zacchaeus in the tree (Luke 19:5) — confirms that the divine watching is not passive surveillance but active, relational knowing.
4: Type. "A wholesome tongue is a tree of life" — the tongue that gives life is a type of Christ who IS the tree of life (Revelation 2:7; Proverbs 22:2, 14). His words are not merely wholesome but life-constituting: "The words I have spoken to you are spirit and they are life" (John 6:63). The twisted tongue that crushes the spirit corresponds to the "old serpent" whose speech in Eden was the original life-crushing deception. The contrast between the life-giving tongue and the spirit-crushing tongue is the contrast between Christ's speech and the adversary's.
5: Theme. Christ demonstrates in his own person the "good sense" of accepting correction. He "learned obedience from what he suffered" (Hebrews 5:8) — the discipline of submission to the Father's will, even unto death. Where the fool rejects the Father's discipline (as Israel repeatedly did), Christ the obedient Son receives it fully. This does not imply that Christ had failings to be corrected, but that his formation through suffering was the path of wisdom-through-discipline that this verse commends: accepting correction rather than rejecting it as a threat.
6: Theme. "The household of the righteous holds great treasure" — the great treasure of the righteous household is Christ himself, who is "wisdom from God, and righteousness and sanctification and redemption" (1 Corinthians 1:30) and in whom "are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge" (Colossians 2:3). The household he builds (Matthew 16:18) holds the greatest possible treasure: the people of God who are his inheritance (Ephesians 1:18) and in whom he dwells (Colossians 1:27). The wicked's trouble corresponds to the poverty of any life oriented around lesser goods.
7: Theme. "The lips of the wise spread knowledge" — Christ's evangelistic commission extends the wisdom tradition's knowledge-spreading to the ends of the earth. "Go and make disciples of all nations" (Matthew 28:19) is the supreme sending of wise lips to spread the knowledge of God. The disciples' preaching is itself an extension of Christ's wisdom-spreading lips (Acts 1:1 — what Jesus "began to do and to teach" continues through the apostolic community). The Spirit's work of bringing to remembrance "everything I have said to you" (John 14:26) sustains the spreading.
8: Type. "The LORD detests the sacrifice of the wicked, but the prayer of the upright is his delight" — the sacrificial distinction that this verse articulates is resolved in Christ. His sacrifice is the one acceptable offering that the sacrifices of the wicked could never be: "By one sacrifice he has made perfect forever those who are being made holy" (Hebrews 10:14). The detested sacrifices of the old system — when offered by the wicked or as a substitute for obedience (Isaiah 1:11–15) — are replaced by Christ's self-offering. Simultaneously, his intercession as the upright one is the prayer that the Father eternally delights in.
9: Revelation of God. "He loves whoever earnestly pursues righteousness" — Christ is both the object of the Father's love ("This is my Son, whom I love, in whom I am well pleased," Matthew 3:17) and the one through whom earnest pursuit of righteousness becomes possible. Paul's declaration that "God made him who had no sin to be sin for us, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God" (2 Corinthians 5:21) makes Christ the path by which those who did not pursue righteousness can now pursue it authentically, receiving both the love this verse promises and the righteousness it rewards.
10: Theme. "There is harsh discipline for the one who leaves the right path, and whoever hates correction will die" — Christ received the harsh discipline that belongs to those who left the right path, though he never left it. Isaiah's servant is described as "stricken by God, smitten by him, and afflicted" (Isaiah 53:4) — bearing the discipline that belongs to the straying. Those who "hate correction" and refuse to return to the path correspond to the final condition of those who reject the gospel. Christ's bearing of the harsh discipline opens the possibility of return for all who will accept correction.
11: Revelation of God. "Death and Destruction lie open before the LORD — how much more the hearts of people!" — Christ descended into the regions of death (Acts 2:27, 31; Ephesians 4:9–10; 1 Peter 3:19) and emerged as the conquering Lord. The divine omniscience that penetrates even Sheol and Abaddon is embodied in Christ who holds "the keys of death and Hades" (Revelation 1:18). His descent into the depths proves that nothing — not even the depths of human corruption or the dimensions of death — lies beyond the LORD's penetrating sight and sovereign reach.
12: Theme. "A mocker does not like being corrected and will not seek the company of the wise" — the mockers who surrounded the cross (Matthew 27:39–44) are the supreme instance: given the presence of wisdom incarnate, they respond with scorn rather than reception. "He came to his own, but his own people did not receive him" (John 1:11) describes the tragically closed community of the mockers. Contrast with those who "seek the company of the wise" — the disciples who drew near to learn, the crowds who pressed to hear — and whose openness was itself the condition of their formation.
13: Revelation of God. "A joyful heart makes a bright face, but a crushed spirit shows in the countenance" — Christ who is "familiar with suffering" and whose face was "marred beyond human likeness" (Isaiah 52:14) in his passion is also the one who promises that his own joy will be in his disciples and "that your joy may be complete" (John 15:11). His countenance, described as shining "like the sun" at the transfiguration (Matthew 17:2) and as the source of the new Jerusalem's light (Revelation 22:5), is the ultimate bright face of the joy-filled heart — a face that bears the marks of suffering transformed into glory.
14: Theme. "A discerning heart is always seeking knowledge" — Christ models the seeking heart that is the disposition of discipleship. "Follow me" (Mark 1:17) is an invitation to the ongoing, active seeking of knowledge that this verse commends. Paul prays that believers would have "the spirit of wisdom and revelation" for "the knowledge of him" (Ephesians 1:17), recognizing that union with Christ does not end seeking but deepens it. The discerning heart that always seeks knowledge is the heart that never exhausts the inexhaustible depths of Christ (cf. Ephesians 3:18–19).
15: Theme. "A joyful heart provides a constant feast" — Paul's instruction to "Rejoice always" (1 Thessalonians 5:16; Philippians 4:4) and the promise of "the peace of God, which transcends all understanding" (Philippians 4:7) constitute the NT version of this proverb's inner feast. Christ himself is the source of this joy: "These things I have spoken to you, that my joy may be in you, and that your joy may be full" (John 15:11). The joyful heart's feast is the feast of union with Christ — the one who turns water into wine (John 2) and who himself is the food and drink that provides inexhaustible nourishment.
16: Revelation of God. "Better to have little with the fear of the LORD than great wealth with turmoil" — Christ's beatitude "Blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven" (Matthew 5:3) and his own life of poverty ("Foxes have dens and birds have nests, but the Son of Man has no place to lay his head," Luke 9:58) embody the better-ness of little-with-God. He is the ultimate demonstration of this proverb: he who had all things (John 17:5) chose poverty and the fear of the Father over the "turmoil" of worldly wealth that the enemy offered (Matthew 4:8–9).
17: Theme. "Better a simple meal where love is present than a lavish feast accompanied by hatred" — Christ's fellowship meals consistently subvert the prestige-economics of ancient banqueting. His table fellowship with tax collectors and sinners (Matthew 9:10–13), the feeding of the multitudes with bread and fish (not a lavish feast), and the Last Supper with bread and wine (simple fare, supreme love) all exemplify the simple-meal-with-love principle. The new covenant's Eucharistic meal is the perpetual enactment of this proverb: simple elements, maximum love.
18: Theme. "Whoever is slow to anger quiets it" — Christ as the peacemaker who quiets strife through his own patience. "Blessed are the peacemakers, for they will be called children of God" (Matthew 5:9) — Christ is the Son of God who is the supreme peacemaker, making peace "through his blood, shed on the cross" (Colossians 1:20). His slowness to anger in the face of maximum provocation (the Passion narrative) is the most powerful possible quieting of strife — it absorbs the violence rather than escalating it.
19: Theme. "The road of the upright is a level highway" — Isaiah applies the level-highway image to the messianic age: "Prepare the way for the LORD; make straight in the wilderness a highway for our God. Every valley shall be raised up, every mountain and hill made low" (Isaiah 40:3–4, cited in Matthew 3:3 of John the Baptist preparing for Christ). Christ is both the one for whom the highway is prepared and the one who himself is "the Way" — the level path that Israel could not walk on its own. John 14:6 is the fulfilment of the level highway Isaiah promised.
20: Revelation of God. "A wise son brings his father joy" — Christ is the Son who brings the Father perfect joy: "This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased" (Matthew 3:17; Proverbs 17:5). The entire gospel narrative moves toward this divine delight — the Father's joy in the Son who does everything the Father gives him to do (John 5:19–20; Proverbs 17:4). Christ fulfills this proverb not as one instance of the wise-son pattern but as its ultimate realization: the Son whose wisdom is perfect brings the Father's joy to its highest expression.
21: Theme. "A person of understanding walks straight" — Christ is the one who walks perfectly straight, the euthys (immediately, directly, straight) of Mark's Gospel expressing his direct, purposeful movement. He sets his face toward Jerusalem (Luke 9:51) — the supreme act of walking straight toward the destination that his mission requires, despite the disciples' resistance and his own human recoiling. His straight walk is the model for the disciple's walk — not meandering but purposeful, oriented by the understanding of where the journey leads.
22: Theme. "With many counselors they succeed" — Christ gathers around himself the community of discernment. He sends the disciples out in pairs (Mark 6:7; Luke 10:1) — built-in counsel. He promises the Spirit as counselor (Paraklete, "one called alongside," John 14:16) who guides into all truth. The wisdom he builds into his community is not the wisdom of one brilliant individual but the distributed wisdom of the body, where "the body does not consist of one part but of many" (1 Corinthians 12:14). The many-counselor principle is the shape of the Spirit-led community.
23: Theme. "A person finds joy in giving the right answer, and how good is a timely word!" — Christ gives the right answer at every moment of his earthly ministry. His answers to the Pharisees' trick questions (Matthew 22:15–40) are celebrated precisely as "the right answer" — leaving his opponents with nothing to say. His promise that the Spirit will "give you words and wisdom that none of your adversaries will be able to resist" (Luke 21:15) extends his own timely-word capacity to his disciples. The joy in giving the right answer is the joy of saying what is needed when it is needed.
24: Theme. "The way of life leads upward for the wise, so that they may avoid Sheol below" — Christ's ascension is the supreme "way upward" — he who descended to the lowest depths (Ephesians 4:9) rose to the highest heights (Ephesians 4:10) and now leads the wise upward by his Spirit. The avoidance of Sheol through wisdom finds its fullest expression in the promise that those who believe will not see death (John 8:51; Proverbs 11:25–26) but will be raised to life. Christ is the wisdom that leads the upward way: "If anyone is in Christ, the new creation has come: The old has gone, the new is here!" (2 Corinthians 5:17).
25: Revelation of God. "The LORD will pull down the house of the proud, but he maintains the widow's property lines" — Christ demonstrates this in his temple action (pulling down the house of pride-turned-commerce, Matthew 21:12–13) and in his specific care for widows (Luke 7:11–17, raising the widow of Nain's son; Luke 21:1–4, commending the widow's offering). The God who maintains the widow's boundary-line is the God who appears in Christ defending the structurally vulnerable — the widow, the poor, the socially marginal — against those who exploit their vulnerability.
26: Revelation of God. "The LORD detests the plans of the wicked, but pure words are pleasing to him" — Christ's speech is supremely "pure words" (ʾimrê ṭāhōr) — speech without admixture of deception, self-interest, or malice. Peter's citation of Isaiah 53:9 ("No deceit was found in his mouth," 1 Peter 2:22) identifies Christ as the living instance of the pure-words principle. His speech pleases the Father (Matthew 3:17; Proverbs 17:5 — the Father speaks his pleasure at the Son's words and works). Every word Christ speaks is the divine pleasure given verbal form.
27: Allusion. "Whoever refuses bribes will live" — Christ refuses the ultimate bribe: the enemy offers him "all the kingdoms of the world and their splendor" (Matthew 4:8–9) in exchange for worship. His refusal ("Away from me, Satan! For it is written: 'Worship the Lord your God, and serve him only,'" Matthew 4:10) is the supreme instance of the bribe-refusal that this verse connects to life. The one who could have taken the kingdoms on easy terms takes them instead through the cross — refusing the corrupt acquisition that would have brought his household trouble.
28: Revelation of God. "The heart of the righteous weighs its answer carefully" — Christ's deliberate, measured speech is one of the most consistent features of the Gospel portraits. He often pauses before answering (John 8:6–8, writing in the sand before responding to the woman caught in adultery). He asks clarifying questions (Mark 10:3, 51; Luke 8:30). He sometimes gives no answer at all (Mark 14:61; Proverbs 15:5). His weighing of answers carefully is the embodiment of the righteous heart that does not blurt but considers. His speech is always appropriate to the moment — a form of wisdom in action.
29: Theme. "He hears the prayer of the righteous" — Christ as our high priest intercedes for the righteous: "He always lives to intercede for them" (Hebrews 7:25). The LORD's hearing of the righteous is mediated through Christ's ongoing priestly presence "at the right hand of God" (Romans 8:34). His own prayer for his disciples (John 17) is the supreme righteous prayer that the Father hears; the disciples' prayers "in my name" (John 14:13–14) are heard because they are incorporated into his priestly intercession. Distance from the wicked / nearness to the righteous is the pattern of the mediating Christ.
30: Theme. "Good news refreshes the whole body" — the gospel (euangelion, good news) is the ultimate refreshment of the body. Isaiah 52:7 ("How beautiful are the feet of those who bring good news!") grounds the NT proclamation of the gospel in this wisdom-tradition recognition that good news has a physically restorative quality. The resurrection of Christ is the best news conceivable — death overcome, life restored, the enemy defeated — and its reception refreshes not only spirits but (in eschatological promise) bodies themselves, as resurrection-hope is the promise of bodily renewal (Philippians 3:21; 1 Corinthians 15:42–44).
31: Theme. "The ear that heeds life-giving correction will find its home among the wise" — the disciples who heeded Christ's often-corrective teaching found their home in the community of the wise — the household of God. He who "will build my church" (Matthew 16:18) is simultaneously the correction-giver whose life-giving rebukes (Peter's denial predicted; Thomas's doubt challenged; the disciples' ambition redirected) form the community that becomes "no longer foreigners and strangers, but fellow citizens with God's people and members of his household" (Ephesians 2:19).
32: Theme. "Whoever accepts reproof gains insight" — the disciples who accepted Christ's correction were formed by it into wisdom. Paul's account of the Christian life as continuous correction by Scripture, useful "for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness" (2 Timothy 3:16), extends the formation-through-accepted-reproof principle to the whole community of the redeemed. Christ does not merely teach insight but gives it through the Spirit (John 16:13), who "will convince the world concerning sin and righteousness and judgment" (John 16:8) — a kind of cosmic reproof that leads to insight for those who accept it.
33: Direct. "The fear of the LORD is training in wisdom, and humility comes before honor" — Paul applies the second clause directly to Christ: "he humbled himself by becoming obedient to death — even death on a cross! Therefore God exalted him to the highest place" (Philippians 2:8–9). The humility-before-honor sequence is Christ's own path — the deepest humiliation in human history followed by the highest exaltation. His exaltation does not contradict his humility but is its consequence: because he did not grasp at equality with God (Philippians 2:6), God gave it to him in the form no one could take away. This is the wisdom that the fear of the LORD produces when taken to its ultimate depth.
